questions.prompt: Why are per-user random salts essential for password storage?

choices.text: Salts defeat rainbow tables and ensure two users with the same password get different hashes. Without salts: standard rainbow tables (precomputed hash → password) crack common passwords instantly; an attacker who cracks 'password123' on one user has cracked it on all users who chose it. With per-user random salts (`hash(salt + password)`): rainbow tables would need to be precomputed per salt (impractical); each password must be attacked individually. Salts must be unique per user (a single global salt = one custom rainbow table for the whole DB) and don't need to be secret (stored alongside the hash). Modern algorithms (bcrypt, argon2) generate and embed salts automatically — implementations that 'forget the salt' or 'use the username as salt' are doing it wrong. Defaults from well-known libraries get this right; manual implementations often get it wrong.

questions.explanation: Per-user salts defeat rainbow tables and force per-password brute force. Bcrypt/argon2 handle automatically.

questions.prompt: Login takes 5ms with bcrypt cost=4. Adjust?

choices.text: Increase cost factor — 5ms is way too fast for password hashing in 2026. At cost=4, an attacker with modern GPUs cracks weak/medium passwords in your database in hours. Target: 100-500ms per hash on your hardware. Adjust cost (each +1 roughly doubles time) until login takes 250ms or so; cost=12-14 is typical on modern servers. The 250ms barely affects users (login happens rarely) but multiplies attacker cost by orders of magnitude. Calibration matters: too slow and your auth API gets DoS'd by brute force; too fast and offline cracking is cheap. Re-calibrate every 1-2 years as hardware improves (or use argon2 with memory_cost, which resists GPU/ASIC better). On the next user login, you can transparently re-hash with the new cost — making this an evolving defense.

questions.explanation: Bcrypt at 5ms is too fast. Increase cost to target 100-500ms per hash. Re-calibrate periodically.
